replies.author: @Undaglibenglaubengloben
replies.text:
@carlosleyva-calistenia6400 I’m sorry for your friend. But if he’s already getting bullied for acting “weird” (and believe me, he’s probably masking 90% of his natural behaviors to pass as neurotypical, as most of us high-functioning autistics do), disclosing the condition only makes it worse. I mask as much as possible when I’m in public and it takes a huge mental load, constantly being hyper aware of everything, consciously observing every input from your surroundings, what face did people make, what context are we in, what is the adequate response, what facial expression should I make, but even with all of that sometimes something slips - I for example sometimes forget to concentrate on my walking, and then I look stiff, and someone notices and makes a comment. My reaction is to play it off (part of my masking includes keeping a huge mental catalog of pre-written responses for all sorts of situations) saying something like “yeah, was leg day yesterday and now everything hurts hahaha”. 
It’s a lot of work and will make my life shorter, but it’s better than the alternative.

replies.author: @Undaglibenglaubengloben
replies.text: @carlosleyva-calistenia6400 but I disagree in one thing. As a high functioning autistic, it doesn’t make my life hell - on the contrary, it gives me all sorts of super powers that I can imagine living without. What makes my life hell are the nasty neurotypicals and their ignorance. Because what they consider “weird” and bully us for, is just different. We autistics get along with each other just fine - and by the way, we find neurotypicals to be the weird ones, and when we’re among us we make fun of their BS social rituals too. I think if we were the majority, they would be the ones being bullied.

replies.author: @SailorDonut
replies.text:
@Orius25 It's not a filter thing. Filter means saying what you're really thinking without concern for social norms. With coprolalia, the brain is latching onto exactly what your subconscious knows is the worst thing you could say, and forcing it out against your will. It's usually accompanied by anxiety, such as, "oh no, what if I said this horrible thing," and the disorder goes "okay sounds cool, here we go!" Similar to intrusive thoughts you may get with an anxiety disorder or OCD, but beyond just a fear or even a compulsion, it's an involuntary motor dysfunction that forces it out. The only way to stop it would be to keep people with coprolalia from knowing bad words in the first place, which is impossible the older someone gets without segregating them from society. And you're not just going to hear these words in COD lobbies. Not to out myself as sheltered or anything, but I had never heard the word in question in my life until I got into high school and had to read a book about racism, wherein they TAUGHT me the word. So this guy might not have even picked it up from somebody racistly using the word, he might have learned it in school, whereupon his subconscious stored it away for his disorder to latch onto as soon as the worst possible occasion arose. 

EDIT: TL;DR: Basically, lack of filter implies you're involuntarily telling the truth/what you believe to be true, like in Liar Liar. Whereas with this condition, you're involuntarily saying what you know is BAD.

replies.text: @BrentWilliams2 This is inane. Assault (or more completely, assault and battery) is a crime which requires mens rea, or intent. Tics lack intent. He didn't 'verbally assault' anyone. It's like saying that someone who has an illness that might cause them to collapse and collide with someone, such as narcolepsy, epilepsy, or even heart disease, or even someone who is just clumsy, ought to be confined or excluded as a 'danger to others'. At worst, this incident is the verbal equivalent of tripping and shoving someone. Or the brakes failing on a wheelchair. You might be upset - until you realise it wasn't intentional, and laugh it off. You might even ask the guy who ran into you if he was alright. The hosts did everything right - keep calm, carry on, and issue a clear explanation. There should have been no problem.

replies.author: @noone1929
replies.text:
Reminds me of last year when I finally decided to actually get tested for a learning disability because when I first heard of it I was like “Wow, that sounds really relatable, but this could just all be in my head”. And I’m still figuring things out, but at my last job (I did end up leaving) after testing showed I do have it, I hesitated to try and explain why I was struggling with certain “easy” tasks. When I finally did, while my coworkers said they’d be supportive, but one of the first things my boss said after I tried to give an example was “Oh, everyone does that”. 
And it’s like sure to some extent, but that comment really doesn’t make me feel included, it feels like belittling my struggles that I’m only just realizing aren’t all in my head or that I’m just profoundly stupid which as undiagnosed in school is how I felt for a lot of classes.
I will probably be spending my whole life figuring out how I think, but comments like that really don’t help.

replies.author: @anglowelshdragon4179
replies.text: @noone1929 There is no neuro normal.   We are all different, we all diverge from one in another in how we think and perceive the world.  Stop wasting your life trying to explain everything about yourself. You are you for all you flaws and quirks.  It has no bearing on the essential things  ,i.e.  how you treat people and your moral character.  Have a good life and stop navel gazing.  And if you have traits that are impeding you at work or in your personal life stop making excuses and find workarounds or learn to do better, It is what every other generation in history did before we became obsessed with self examination and therapy.

​​@niles536 Here's an example that may help you understand, as it did for me.  The One Show once featured a teacher with Tourette's (which was quite explicit) but her pupils were great, very mature and able to filter it out, and she seemed to be very effective and popular. She then revealed other difficult moments, including visiting an airport just after 9/11 and finding herself shouting 'bomb!' 

That's when it clicked for me, her treacherous mind* had unconsciously absorbed the Very Worst Thing To Say at that time in our culture, and of course it broke free when stressed. It's the exact same thing happening here.

*Which we all have, btw (to one degree or another), and would be far happier admitting!

replies.author: @ryancampbell2192
replies.text:
I had a teaching assistant/graduate student who assisted my professor in an upper level medieval history class in college.
The guy had tourette's, including verbal & a few physical ticks.
He had to be careful with the rare books...he was fine with his right arm, but had learned that his left arm would spasm infrequently, but uncontrollably & he had damaged a lot of his books over the years that way.
He unfortunately would also bark like a dog periodically & frequently said "cunt, cunt".
He was the sweetest guy, gangly & nerdish and funny as hell. He had a bookish girlfriend who was ready to throw down when people would get offended or verbally attack him.
It's been 35 years now since I graduated college...I hope they stayed together & are well. Gotta be a tough life.

no. you got it the wrong way. These people THINK they are woke, empathetic and progressive, while displaying the exact form of ignorance and entitlement that people are supposed to be "awoken" from. I.e: she's not woke enough. Being woke or feminist usually comes with being informed - or it should - and this is clearly a misinformed, entitled and judgmental individual and consequently cannot be labelled as woke in the actual sense. It's what you do, not what you label yourself as. This is false advertisement - and appropriation.. but let's not delve into culture wars and fights for interpretation and who is in the in-group and who isn't. It's a moot point. The film was meant to "awaken" people or better "make aware" and educate - good ol' words with actual meaning... - so precisely this kind of misjudgment won't happen.

I hope she watches the film - though she might not enjoy herself as half the dialogue will be bleeped - and see that she "cannot understand neurodivergent thinking" and get better... 'cause.. that's the whole point... becoming better... not 'being ok one already is with no personal growth whatsoever' though that's the same issue with different pathology...

Genuine question. What is it if not a mental illness/condition?

replies.author: @Truffalot
replies.text: ​​@TheGodOfGravyIt's neurodevelopmental. This means it starts in childhood and affects how the brain develops. So it isn't a mental illness, it's physical changes to the brain because of development differences. It's the same category as Autism, dyslexia, intellectual disabilities, down syndrome, etc.

replies.author: @chillax319
replies.text: @Truffalot True but aren't many mental ilnesses caused by problems with the physical structure of the brain/hormonal inbalances?

replies.author: @8light7

replies.author: @Truffalot
replies.text:
​@chillax319The key difference is that one is developed from birth and the other isn't. Let me explain with a few different things. If your parent abuses you and you grow up depressed, yes your brain will change due to trauma. But it isn't a developmental change that's part of you from birth.

With chemical and hormone changes relating to depression, a lot of that is caused by your environment. However some are caused by genetics, in which case it's a genetic disorder. It's usually not something caused by neurodevelopment. Remember your hormones aren't made in the brain. Either way, if you are depressed and it's impacted by a neurodevelopmental disorder, then you have a mental illness *and* a developmental disorder. It's not one or the other.

In the same fashion that if you have chronic pain you are likely to be depressed. That doesn't mean your chronic pain is a mental illness. 

Another key difference is that neurodevelopmental disorders are permanent in the brain. That's how your brain was developed and it *can't* be cured. Mental illness *can* go away, even if it comes back. It's in the brain, but it isn't how your brain development instructions are. It can be changed. 

The key differences are that it occurs since birth, is lifelong, and is because of the brain's development. Tourette's is because of brain development. It can't be caused by life experiences (except for maybe severe brain injuries). It can't be fixed. It isn't anybody's fault.

Side note: Depression "caused by" chemical imbalances is a bit of a myth. There's such an extreme amount more that goes into it that just having more of less of a certain chemical.

replies.author: @Truffalot
replies.text:
​​@kenknight5983There's a big misconception about chemical imbalances. Depression is not caused by an imbalance, but depression medication does work. The idea of depression being caused by imbalances has been spread so much because it allowed people to say "it's because of your biology" rather than understanding how depression works and what actually causes it. 

In 2022 there was a massive review showing there is no consistent evidence relating serotonin levels to depression. Having lower or higher serotonin levels did not have any link to whether a person would or wouldn't have depression. 

Serotonin is not like a vitamin or alcohol. You can get an iron deficiency and that causes specific symptoms. You can have very low or very high serotonin and not have it affect your mood. Taking serotonin is not like taking a vitamin tablet. In this study they even looked at the genes behind serotonin and found that gene variations did not change depression rates either, nor did differences in effectiveness of serotonin receptors.

The reason taking SSRI medication helps after a few weeks is because it's not just patching a deficiency. The thing is scientists know that SSRI can work, and they know that it isn't because of simple chemical imbalances, but they don't know 100% how it works. This is the same with most medications actually. They can't exactly cut open a brain as it's functioning and study how it's working.

The two main theories as to how it works are: 
Reduces brain inflammation: Brain inflammation theory is gaining more traction as scientists are proving that it links directly to depression, and SSRI reduces it. 
Increases neuroplasticity: Helps the brain with it's ability to develop and change new connections, thoughts, patterns etc. That's why therapy + medication is more effective. 

So yeah, the whole "my serotonin level is lower than most people" is just not a cause of depression. People with average and high serotonin get depression the same amount. Yes this does compare serotonin levels against other people AND against your other chemical levels. There are many other things that go into it and it isn't just a chemical imbalance.

Stuff like this realy makes my blood boil.

I worked with people with down syndrome (i had such a good and fun time) for several years and we had a local politician who was all about the diversity and inclusion talk all day.
We then had a gathering promoting awarness and stuff and she showed up too.
And damn i have never seen a person with such disgust in her face before, when she was forced to stand near people with down syndrome and shake their hands, she also left after a few minutes already.

For many people its just talk to look good, but they have zero knowledge about the topic and often dont realy give a single fuck about it in reality.

And nowdays you can find these people in droves on social media.
Iam glad YT is the only plattform i use, so i dont have to witness most of it.

replies.author: @Rishi123456789
replies.text:
​@EricA-xd9fn I get what you're saying and you have made a valid point. I think John Davidson should've never attended in the first place. I don't remember the full story about him, but if he really wanted to have some kind of presence at the show, he could have appeared in a pre-recorded message talking about how glad he was to have a movie based on him or whatever. A pre-recorded message would have avoided the whole involuntary tic thing. A pre-recorded message is not segregation because it still allows him to participate in the award show without him disrupting that award show because of his involuntary tics. I'm unironically with the woke weirdos on this one and that's saying something because I almost never agree with them on anything. There is no valid excuse for this. People who are involuntarily disruptive in an upsetting type of way like John Davidson have no place in polite formal settings like award shows. I'm hearing now that John Davidson not only said that racial slur, but also made a sexist comment to a woman and also made a homophobic slur to someone else all at the same award show. I admit that I don't know whether this is true or not, but if it's true then it seems like he has certain bigoted views that have nothing to do with Tourette syndrome and he's simply using Tourette syndrome as a shield to protect himself from legitimate criticism because remember, Tourette syndrome has nothing to do with your views toward black people, women and gay people. And another thing I don't like about this scandal is that the BBC censored the words "Free Palestine" (a sentiment embraced by literally billions of people), but didn't censor an actual racial slur. That's pure hypocrisy and that seems like a deliberate attempt to distract people from the Epstein files. 

Mentally ill people should be treated with compassion, but they also shouldn't be allowed to participate in polite society if they can't behave in a way that isn't aggressively disruptive. Being mentally ill is not a valid excuse for being an obnoxious asshole. And before you judge me for this comment of mine, I have a mental disorder myself (obsessive-compulsive disorder) but I still don't behave in an aggressively disruptive way.

author: @AdamWallis26
text:
I have Tourette's myself, my dad has it and its a herreditary thing. The feeling of not doing a tic is less like a demon on your shoulder, its more like trying not to blink. Yeah you can stop it for a while but it gets really uncomfortable after even a few minutes, and half the time you're not even really aware that you're doing it

The reason he used that word isnt because he wanted to, its because the thought of "oh God whats the worst thing i could say?" Entered his head and its impossible to try NOT to think about something

replies.author: @kevinh1178
replies.text: Thank you for that clear and honest explanation ❤

replies.author: @SAVANNAHDUKE
replies.text: As someone who also has Tourette’s, I definitely agree with the blink thing. When I was younger, I had no control over it, but now that I’m older, I can sometimes delay the tic for a minute, but it definitely has that uncomfortable trying not blink feeling and it tends to just make it worse when I do tic. And I also agree, it does often times happen as “what do I not want to do the most” and your Tourette’s is like “do that”.

replies.author: @Content_Deleted
replies.text:
Can confirm that intrusive thoughts mixed with Tourette's is absolutely awful. To pretend that couldn't be a possibility is to deny a human fundamental. For some people it just pops into their head out of nowhere, for literally no reason. And some people will screech and claw and insist there is a reason.

Yeah, the reason is they're black. The brain connects things. Separate parts of our brains handle language, and social consequence. Doesn't make the word okay. Doesn't make saying it okay. But the fact it came out was INVOLUNTARY, and how some of these people can be so stupid to ignore that fact is just insane. Michael and Delroy moved on with their speech. Someone came and clarified, apologized.

But that's the thing about the internet. There's always some idiots who won't LET it be. Who feel like something needs to be added. Or clarified. Need to speak down to someone to validate themselves. That kind of insanity is just beyond reason.

replies.author: @JadeRunner
replies.text: ​@spindriftdrinker I assume you don't watch South Park. The faker in the episode, Cartman, is well established in the show as a colossal scumbag with no depths that he won't stoop to in order to exploit something for his own gain. The show depicting Cartman as faking tourette's was not the show implying people actually fake tourette's in real life - quite the opposite. It's precisely because faking tourette's is such an unthinkable, absurd concept that showing Cartman doing it is hilarious, and fitting to his character.

Humor is one of the best ice breakers, if they had just lectured us the entire time it would’ve had a substantially more negative effect on the Tourette’s community.

author: @TDubbs00
text:
My son has Tourettes. I’m so grateful he did not grovel to the mob. Imagine what that would have done for everyone he has tried to help & advocate for by putting himself out there and telling his story?
  It is a complicated thing that is hard for people to understand. For those who think it is within their control, my son had a spinning tic that got so bad he would run into things & hurt himself.
  If you ever find yourself wondering if a vocal tic has an element of desire mixed in, it might help to think of the physical tics you’re are likely witnessing in tandem.  It’s obvious those movements are not by choice. Vocal & physical tics are a result of the same neurological mechanism.

author: @andrewpetik2034
text:
As a father of a child who has been diagnosed as being afflicted with Tourette's..... an affliction that led to our family being asked to leave our church of a decade.

It is deeply troubling,  it is incredibly embarrassing for the family AND  definitely the person afflicted AND it is socially crippling....and there is absolutely NO way to control it. 

I have so much i want to say, but I will  only add..... if you want to promote inclusion, be truly informed about these ACTUAL disabilities. 
Tourette's, Asberger's, Autism, etc.

Thanks Drinker for bringing  attention to this truly challenging issue.
🙏💪💪✌️💪💪🙏

Edit....our child has the verbal and facial tics.... no offensive language....thank goodness
